title: OAuth-token plumbing into MCP is dead end-to-end with false docstrings; cost paid every chat turn and resume
claim: get_user_oauth_tokens is fetched in chat_view and the resume task and passed to build_agent_graph whose oauth_tokens param is referenced only in the signature/docstring and never read; config['oauth_tokens'] is set top-level (not under configurable, so LangGraph neither injects into tool _meta nor persists it) and read by nothing; extract_oauth_tokens has zero non-test callers and its docstring falsely claims transport-layer injection; TenantContext.oauth_tokens is never populated or read. get_user_oauth_tokens also filters to COMMCARE_PROVIDERS and excludes OCS, which reads like a bug but is inert since the whole transport is dead. Real credentials flow worker-side via aresolve_credential/TenantConnection. ~5 files of misleading vestige actively maintained through the fix chain; tokens may be persisted into LangGraph checkpoint rows for no benefit.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain from every cited file.

1) build_agent_graph (apps/agents/graph/base.py): `oauth_tokens` appears ONLY at line 485 (signature `oauth_tokens: dict | None = None,`) and line 495 (docstring `oauth_tokens: Optional OAuth tokens for tool authentication.`). The function body runs 480-668 (next def `_build_tools` at line 668); `awk '/oauth/'` over 480-700 returns only those two lines. Never read in the body. CONFIRMED.

2) Top-level config['oauth_tokens'] is set in apps/chat/views.py:196 and apps/workspaces/tasks.py:1154, but it is OUTSIDE `configurable` (views.py:194 `"configurable": {"thread_id": thread_id}`; tasks.py:1152 same shape). A full grep for `.get("oauth_tokens")` / `["oauth_tokens"]` / `configurable...oauth` across the repo (minus tests) returns exactly ONE hit: mcp_server/auth.py:25 `return meta.get("oauth_tokens", {})` — which reads MCP `_meta`, a different and also-dead path. Nothing reads the config key. CONFIRMED.

3) extract_oauth_tokens (mcp_server/auth.py:13-25): `grep -rln extract_oauth_tokens` returns only its own file and tests/test_mcp_server.py. mcp_server/server.py never imports mcp_server.auth; tool handlers resolve context via `_resolve_mcp_context` -> load_workspace_context/load_tenant_context which return QueryContext (mcp_server/server.py:48, services/query.py:16, services/metadata.py:19). No production caller injects oauth_tokens into `_meta` either (only reference to MCP `_meta` + oauth is in auth.py's own docstring). The auth.py module docstring's claim that tokens are 'injected by the Django chat view at the transport layer' is false — no such injection exists. CONFIRMED.

4) TenantContext (mcp_server/context.py:35, oauth_tokens field at line 42): `grep -rln 'import TenantContext|TenantContext('` returns NOTHING. Never imported, never constructed. The test class `TestLoadTenantContext` (tests/test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:523) actually asserts on a QueryContext ('Test that load_tenant_context builds the correct QueryContext'). So TenantContext.oauth_tokens is never populated or read. CONFIRMED.

5) COMMCARE_PROVIDERS exclusion of OCS: mcp_client.py:76 `COMMCARE_PROVIDERS = frozenset({"commcare", "commcare_connect"})`; get_user_oauth_tokens (mcp_client.py:79-90) filters `account__provider__in=COMMCARE_PROVIDERS`. 'ocs' is a real SocialToken provider (apps/users/signals.py:69, auth_views.py:182-200), so OCS tokens are silently dropped — but inert because the whole transport is dead. CONFIRMED.

Reachability: chat_view is a live route (`path("", chat_view, name="chat")` in apps/chat/urls.py:28 and config/urls.py) → every chat turn runs get_user_oauth_tokens (a SocialToken DB query, views.py:162) and discards the result into dead params. resume_thread_after_materialization is a live Procrastinate task deferred via .defer_async() (tasks.py:393, :804) → _build_agent_for_resume (tasks.py:852/858) does the same. Real credentials flow worker-side via materializer/aresolve_credential, not this path. CONFIRMED.
votes.notes:
One refinement to the finding's only hedged sub-claim. The finding says tokens 'may be persisted into LangGraph checkpoint rows for no benefit.' In practice they are NOT persisted: oauth_tokens is set at the TOP LEVEL of the runtime config, not inside `configurable` (which holds only thread_id). LangGraph's checkpointer persists the `configurable` namespace, so the top-level key is dropped, not written. The finding already correctly hedges this with 'may be,' so this doesn't alter the verdict — it just resolves the hedge toward 'not persisted,' which slightly reduces (not increases) the harm. Everything else is exactly as described.

Per-turn cost is real but small: one indexed SocialToken query returning a usually-empty dict, threaded through ~5 files of dead params/docstrings. Correctly DEBT/velocity — no runtime breakage, no security exposure via this path (the audit-log scrub at mcp_server/envelope.py:82 and the _meta extraction are themselves dead). The actively-maintained false docstrings (auth.py module docstring + build_agent_graph param doc) are the main velocity tax, as claimed. replication=6 and the DEBT/velocity classification stand.

title: Two/three materialization-cancel mechanisms with diverging semantics; MCP cancel_materialization writes FAILED not CANCELLED and never stops the load
claim: The API cancel view writes CANCELLED + procrastinate abort + ThreadJob flip; the worker's per-page cancellation checkpoint raises only on state==CANCELLED. The MCP cancel_materialization tool instead sets state=FAILED with result.cancelled=True, never aborts the procrastinate job and never touches the ThreadJob, so a mid-LOAD cancel does NOT stop loading — every page/source completes at full provider-API cost; the LOADING->TRANSFORMING CAS then misses and overwrites result, the run ends terminal FAILED with all sources committed, and downstream aggregation reports 'failed' not 'cancelled' (wrong resume message). Catalog only surfaces COMPLETED/PARTIAL runs, so the fully-loaded data presents as 'no data'. cancel_materialization and get_materialization_status also take an unscoped LLM-supplied run_id with no workspace/user binding (info disclosure: status leaks tenant external_id + physical schema name; cancel any in-flight run); HTTP equivalents scope to thread__user. Reviewers disagree whether these tools are in MCP_TOOL_NAMES/bound to the LLM; practical reachability is limited because no tool returns run_id post fire-and-ack.
chain: agent cancel_materialization(run_id) -> mcp_server/server.py:479 run.state=FAILED -> apps/workspaces/tasks.py:485-494 updater raises only on CANCELLED -> load continues -> materializer.py:435-443 CAS miss overwrites result, run FAILED with committed data -> metadata.py:57-67 hides it; contrast apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:22-121 (CANCELLED + abort)

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain and every mechanical link reproduces. CORRECTIONS are to severity/reachability, not the mechanism.

(1) MCP cancel writes FAILED, not CANCELLED, and stops nothing. mcp_server/server.py:478-482: `run.state = MaterializationRun.RunState.FAILED; run.completed_at = datetime.now(UTC); run.result = {**(run.result or {}), "cancelled": True}; await run.asave(update_fields=["state","completed_at","result"])`. No `current_app.job_manager.cancel_job_by_id_async(...)`, no ThreadJob touch. The `in_progress` guard (server.py:465-476) allows STARTED/DISCOVERING/LOADING/TRANSFORMING.

(2) Worker checkpoint only reacts to CANCELLED. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:485-494 `updater`: `current_state = MaterializationRun.objects.filter(id=run_id).values_list("state", flat=True).first(); if current_state == MaterializationRun.RunState.CANCELLED: raise MaterializationCancelled()`. FAILED is not CANCELLED → the per-page checkpoint never raises, so `_load_and_commit_source` keeps fetching/committing pages at full provider-API cost. `RunState.FAILED="failed"` vs `CANCELLED="cancelled"` confirmed in apps/workspaces/models.py:62-71.

(3) LOADING→TRANSFORMING CAS misses and overwrites result. mcp_server/services/materializer.py:435-443: `rows = MaterializationRun.objects.filter(id=run.id, state=RunState.LOADING).update(state=RunState.TRANSFORMING); if not rows: MaterializationRun.objects.filter(id=run.id).update(result={"cancelled": True, "sources": source_results}); raise MaterializationCancelled()`. Because the MCP cancel already set state=FAILED, the CAS filter (state==LOADING) matches 0 rows; the run STAYS FAILED (the fallback updates only `result`, not `state`), with every committed source preserved in `source_results`.

(4) Aggregation reports failed, not cancelled. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:991-1005: `if r.state == CANCELLED: any_cancelled=True ... elif r.state == FAILED: any_failed=True`; then `if any_cancelled: status="cancelled" elif any_failed: status="failed"`. So the FAILED run yields the wrong resume status ("failed", not "cancelled").

(5) Catalog hides the fully-loaded data. mcp_server/services/metadata.py:57-67 `pipeline_list_tables` filters `state__in=[COMPLETED, PARTIAL]` only; docstring line 36-37 explicitly says "STALE, FAILED, and CANCELLED runs are ignored." A terminal-FAILED run with committed tables surfaces as zero tables → "no data."

(6) Unscoped run_id / info disclosure. server.py:408-442 (get_materialization_status) and 446-493 (cancel_materialization) both take only `run_id: str` and do a global `MaterializationRun.objects.select_related(...).aget(id=run_id)` with NO workspace/user/tenant filter. Status returns `tenant_id = run.tenant_schema.tenant.external_id` and `schema = run.tenant_schema.schema_name` (server.py:426-439) — leaks tenant external_id and physical schema name for any run UUID. Cancel can flip any in-flight run.

(7) HTTP equivalent IS scoped (contrast holds). apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:46-68 filters runs by `tenant_schema__tenant__in=workspace.tenants.all()` and ThreadJobs by `thread__user=user`, sets state=CANCELLED (line 104), and aborts procrastinate via `cancel_job_by_id_async(..., abort=True)` (106-111).

(8) The tools ARE bound to the LLM (resolving the "reviewers disagree" caveat against the finding's favor). apps/agents/mcp_client.py:51-55 `MultiServerMCPClient(...).get_tools()` returns ALL `@mcp.tool()` tools with no allowlist; apps/agents/graph/base.py:692 `_build_tools` does `tools = list(mcp_tools)` (no filter); base.py:407-410 `_llm_tool_schemas` returns any tool NOT in MCP_TOOL_NAMES UNCHANGED, and base.py:521-522 `bind_tools(llm_tool_schemas)`. So cancel_materialization and get_materialization_status are bound to the LLM with their raw `run_id` schema exposed, and base.py:460 (injecting node) skips them since they're outside MCP_TOOL_NAMES — confirming no workspace/user scoping is injected.
votes.notes: PARTIAL (not CONFIRMED) because the agent-driven reachability is materially weaker than replication=9 implies, and the finding's own text concedes this. I verified there is NO in-band path for the LLM to obtain a valid MaterializationRun.run_id: server.py grep shows `run_id` is surfaced only by get_materialization_status (line 431) and cancel_materialization (line 489) themselves — both of which REQUIRE run_id as input (circular). run_materialization's fire-and-ack returns `thread_job_id` (the ThreadJob UUID), not run_id (server.py:640), as does the in-flight guard (595). get_schema_status/list_tables/describe_table do not emit run_id. So for the agent to call these tools it must already possess a run UUID it has no legitimate channel to learn. That makes the agent-triggered cancel-during-LOAD path effectively unreachable in normal operation; the realistic exposure is (a) the info-disclosure/cross-tenant-cancel primitive if a run_id leaks via logs/another surface, and (b) a latent landmine if a future tool ever returns run_id to the LLM. Mechanism and semantics-divergence are fully real and well-documented in code, so this is a genuine LATENT correctness/consistency defect — but the severity/replication should be downgraded from 9 toward ~4-5. corrected_status LATENT (matches finding); the divergence and the missing scoping are real today, they just aren't being exercised. One more nit: the finding says cancel 'overwrites result' on the CAS miss — accurate, but note it overwrites only `result`, leaving state=FAILED, which is precisely why aggregation says 'failed' (the run was never CANCELLED). That detail strengthens rather than weakens the finding.

title: ThreadJob created after defer_async (dispatch/creation race); resume lifecycle is a long fix-chain held together by hedge + janitor
claim: run_materialization (and the retry endpoint, duplicating the sequence) defers the procrastinate job before creating the ThreadJob with no shared transaction; the worker hedges with a bounded ~3.75s sleep-poll (_defer_resume_for_job) and otherwise leans on the 15-min janitor, giving a late-visible ThreadJob a phantom spinner up to ~10-25 min. tasks.py:373 TODO documents the clean fix (pre-create with nullable procrastinate_job_id; migration explicitly skipped). The resume/janitor lifecycle is the repo's largest fix-chain (~15-19 commits); invariants live only as CAS filter arguments and scattered comments rather than one owned module. If ThreadJob.acreate AND the rollback abort both fail (both swallowed), the materialization runs fully orphaned — expire_stale_thread_jobs iterates ThreadJobs only, so nothing reconciles it.
chain: mcp_server/server.py:606-635 defer_async then ThreadJob.acreate -> apps/workspaces/tasks.py:363-396 hedge + janitor fallback; TODO at tasks.py:373; duplicated at apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:184-210

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain and every load-bearing claim holds.

1. DISPATCH/CREATION RACE (no shared txn): mcp_server/server.py:606-635 — `job = await materialize_workspace.defer_async(...)` (line 607) commits the procrastinate job on its own connection, then `tj = await ThreadJob.objects.acreate(...)` (line 623) runs as a SEPARATE Django ORM write. No `transaction.atomic` wraps the two. The code's own comment at server.py:617-621 states: "defer_async and ThreadJob.acreate are not in a single transaction. If acreate fails after the worker has already picked up the job, abort=True is best-effort... The janitor task (expire_stale_thread_jobs) cleans up any orphaned runs."

2. HEDGE ~3.75s: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:380 — `for delay in (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0)` with `await asyncio.sleep(delay)`. Sum of non-zero delays = 0.25+0.5+1.0+2.0 = 3.75s exactly. Docstring at tasks.py:366-371 confirms: "MCP commits the ThreadJob row *after* defer_async returns... under load the worker may finish before the row is visible. Hedge with a bounded backoff: total budget ~3.75s... If the row still is not visible after retries, the janitor catches up eventually."

3. TODO documenting clean fix + skipped migration: tasks.py:373-376 verbatim — "TODO: a cleaner fix is to let MCP write a placeholder ThreadJob *before* defer_async... That requires making procrastinate_job_id nullable (a migration we are skipping for this PR)." The field is still non-nullable: apps/chat/models.py:75 `procrastinate_job_id = models.BigIntegerField(unique=True, db_index=True)` — no `null=True`.

4. JANITOR 15-min cron + 10-min threshold: tasks.py:819 `@app.periodic(cron="*/15 * * * *")`, tasks.py:690 `STALE_JOB_THRESHOLD = timedelta(minutes=10)`. expire_stale_thread_jobs filters `created_at__lt=cutoff` (now-10min). Phantom-spinner worst case: hedge misses the late row, then janitor needs created_at older than 10min AND must wait for the next */15 tick → up to ~10+15 = ~25min. Claim's "~10-25 min" is accurate.

5. ORPHAN: BOTH-fail path = server.py:630-635: acreate raises → logged → `contextlib.suppress(Exception)` around `cancel_job_by_id_async(job_id, abort=True)` → if abort also raises it is swallowed → error returned with NO ThreadJob persisted. Both reconcilers iterate ThreadJobs ONLY and therefore cannot see the orphaned procrastinate job: (a) janitor tasks.py:828-834 `async for tj in ThreadJob.objects...filter(state__in=ACTIVE_STATES, created_at__lt=cutoff)`; (b) API backstop jobs_views.py:101-115/149 also queries `ThreadJob.objects.filter(...)`. Confirmed nothing reconciles an orphaned run.

6. DUPLICATION: apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:184-210 repeats the identical defer_async→job_id-extract→acreate→suppress(cancel abort=True) sequence, with the same non-transactional gap.

7. FIX-CHAIN SIZE: `git log --oneline --all -- apps/workspaces/tasks.py | grep -iE 'resume|janitor|thread.?job|stale|reconcile|...'` returns 30 matches; the visible sample contains ~16 distinct resume/janitor fix commits (ee1018a add resume task, c8b3167 chain resume, 8d407b2 janitor 15min cron, 2fc7e45 tighten resume CAS, 9818e4c dedupe+janitor robustness, e451c7a unblock resume, a18e253 bound resume timeout, 28b6647 janitor FutureApp fix, etc.). Supports "largest fix-chain (~15-19 commits)."

REACHABILITY (both live today): run_materialization is a registered agent tool — apps/agents/graph/base.py:71 lists "run_materialization"; surfaced to users via slash command (frontend/src/components/ChatPanel/slashCommands.ts:23) and rendered as a live card (frontend/src/components/ChatMessage/ChatMessage.tsx:131). The retry endpoint is wired to a live route at apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:29-33 (`materialize/retry/` → materialization_retry_view) and called from the frontend at frontend/src/api/jobs.ts:60.
votes.notes: Status DEBT and impact correctness are both appropriate. The mechanism is the visibility race (Django write becomes visible after the worker already finished and called _defer_resume_for_job), not a generic crash; the finding describes this correctly. Two minor calibrations, neither changing the verdict: (1) The common-case phantom spinner is self-healing within ~25min (janitor) and the API backstop poll (jobs_views.py:117-135) shortens it whenever the frontend is actively polling and the row IS visible — so the most-frequent failure mode degrades gracefully, not silently forever. (2) The truly-unrecoverable "fully orphaned" case is genuinely real but narrow: it requires acreate to fail AND the abort to fail, both swallowed — i.e. two coincident failures (DB write error + procrastinate cancel error). That is a low-probability tail, which is consistent with a DEBT (not BROKEN-NOW) classification. The architectural critique — that this lifecycle's invariants live only as CAS filter arguments (e.g. CLAIMABLE_STATES at tasks.py:1043-1047) and scattered comments rather than one owned module, and that the clean pre-create fix is documented-but-deferred — is accurate and the core of the finding.

title: prune_messages is dead code: conversation history replayed unbounded every LLM call, no trimming/summarization/tool-result compaction
claim: prune_messages/DEFAULT_MAX_MESSAGES in agents/graph/state.py have zero callers and the graph applies no cap (recursion_limit caps tool iterations, not history); the docstring advertises a window strategy that does not run. agent_node sends the full message list every call, and query tool results (up to 500 rows of JSON) enter history verbatim and are re-sent on every later LLM call. Per-turn input tokens grow linearly with thread age, lifetime cost ~quadratically, and long threads eventually overflow the context window and fail with a generic error. Compounds the no-prompt-caching cost.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain by reading each file, not trusting the finding's quotes.

DEAD CODE: `grep -rn "prune_messages\|DEFAULT_MAX_MESSAGES"` over the whole repo returns ONLY apps/agents/graph/state.py — the definition (line 24), the constant (line 21), and a docstring example (line 47). Zero call sites. The function's docstring (state.py:28-49) advertises a window strategy ("Keeps the most recent N messages (default 20)") that never executes.

STATE HAS NO CAP: state.py:138 declares `messages: Annotated[list[BaseMessage], add_messages]` — the stock append/dedupe-by-ID reducer. No trimming reducer is attached.

FULL HISTORY SENT EVERY CALL: agent_node (base.py:538-582) does `state_messages = list(state["messages"])`, removes only prior SystemMessages (line 547), repairs dangling tool_calls, prepends one SystemMessage, then `response = await llm_with_tools.ainvoke(messages)` (line 581). No trim/summarize/compaction. `grep` for trim_messages/RemoveMessage/summariz/prune across chat/views.py and workspaces/tasks.py returns nothing.

recursion_limit IS NOT A HISTORY CAP: views.py:195 sets `"recursion_limit": 50` and tasks.py:1153 sets `AGENT_RESUME_RECURSION_LIMIT`; both bound graph node iterations within a single turn (base.py:6-8 docstring: "A recursion limit prevents runaway loops"), not cross-turn history length.

500-ROW TOOL RESULTS ENTER HISTORY VERBATIM: mcp_server/server.py:334-375 `query()` returns success_response({"columns","rows","row_count",...}); execute_query (services/query.py:106-145) returns full result["rows"]; max limit is `max_rows_per_query=500` (context.py:24,43,77,136). success_response (envelope.py:34-54) wraps `data` with NO truncation. That dict becomes the ToolMessage content, persisted to the Postgres checkpoint and replayed on every later LLM call.

REACHABILITY: chat_view is wired live at /api/chat/ (config/urls.py:98; apps/chat/urls.py:28); it streams via langgraph_to_ui_stream(agent, input_state, config) (views.py:239) over the full checkpointed state. input_state appends only the new HumanMessage (views.py:209-215); prior turns come from the checkpointer. The resume path (tasks.py) shares the same graph and config shape. Both are reachable today.

Net: per-turn input tokens grow with thread age, lifetime cost ~quadratically, and a long enough thread eventually overflows the model context window. The docstring promises a 20-message window that is never wired in.
votes.notes: Minor mechanism nuance, not a refutation: agent_node DOES strip prior SystemMessages each call (base.py:547), so the system prompt isn't duplicated — but that does not bound the Human/AI/Tool history, which is the growth driver. Status LATENT is correct: it does not fail on any single call today; it degrades cost/latency progressively and will fail with a generic error only once a thread is long enough to overflow the context window. Impact cost-perf is correct. The finding's file:line refs all check out (state.py:24-77,138; base.py:538-582; query.py:138; views.py:195). The 500-row figure is the configured max; actual ToolMessage size depends on row width, but the verbatim-no-truncation path is confirmed.

title: Knowledge context injected into every system prompt with no size cap; retriever ignores its relevance argument
claim: KnowledgeRetriever.retrieve(user_question) ignores its argument (never read; sole caller passes nothing) and concatenates ALL KnowledgeEntry rows (full content) and ALL TableKnowledge rows into the system prompt with no budget (only learnings capped at 20), unlike the 6000-char schema budget. With a bulk-import endpoint the prompt can grow arbitrarily and is re-billed on every LLM call. applies_to_tables ('for relevance filtering' per the docstring) is display-only; a duplicated nested '## Knowledge Base' heading appears in every prompt; no zip-bomb/decompressed-size guard on import lets a READ member inflate the section. _build_system_prompt additionally runs pipeline_describe_table per table on every 60s cache miss.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain and reproduced the core mechanism. All substantive claims hold; two ancillary details are inaccurate.

CONFIRMED:
1. Ignored argument: `apps/knowledge/services/retriever.py:33` `async def retrieve(self, user_question: str = "")` — body (lines 34-49) never references `user_question`; it unconditionally formats entries+tables+learnings. Sole caller `apps/agents/graph/base.py:731` `knowledge_context = await retriever.retrieve()` passes nothing.
2. No budget on knowledge: `_format_knowledge_entries` (retriever.py:51-66) iterates `async for entry in entries` over ALL `KnowledgeEntry` rows and appends full `entry.content` (line 63). `_format_table_knowledge` (68-113) iterates ALL `TableKnowledge`. Only `_format_agent_learnings` caps at `MAX_AGENT_LEARNINGS = 20` (line 28/120). The `SCHEMA_CONTEXT_CHAR_BUDGET = 6000` (base.py:81) is applied only in `_fetch_schema_context` (base.py:282), never to the knowledge block (base.py:730-733), confirming the asymmetry.
3. Re-billed every call: `_build_system_prompt` (base.py:699) is called by `build_agent_graph` (base.py:525); graph is rebuilt per chat request (`apps/chat/views.py:167,179`), per background resume (`apps/workspaces/tasks.py:860`), and per recipe run (`apps/recipes/services/runner.py:115`). The result is embedded as `SystemMessage(content=system_prompt)` in `agent_node` (base.py:580) on every `llm_with_tools.ainvoke` — i.e. every turn of the agent loop.
4. Bulk-import with no zip-bomb guard: `KnowledgeImportView.post` (apps/knowledge/api/views.py:271-318) opens the uploaded zip and loops `for name in zf.namelist()` calling `raw = zf.read(name).decode('utf-8')` (line 290) with no decompressed-size limit and no entry-count cap, then `KnowledgeEntry.objects.update_or_create(...)` per file (295). Route is live: `config/urls.py:64` includes `apps.knowledge.urls`, which maps `import/` → `KnowledgeImportView` (apps/knowledge/urls.py). Frontend wired: `frontend/src/store/knowledgeSlice.ts:174` POSTs to `/api/workspaces/${activeDomainId}/knowledge/import/`, triggered from `KnowledgePage.tsx:131`.
5. READ member can inflate: `KnowledgeImportView` uses `resolve_workspace` = `resolve_workspace_drf` (workspace_resolver.py:12-32) which only checks `WorkspaceMembership` existence — NO role check. `WorkspaceRole` defines READ and READ_WRITE (apps/workspaces/models.py:103-105), and the import view (unlike create/edit guards elsewhere) does not gate on READ_WRITE, so a READ member passes.
6. Duplicated '## Knowledge Base' heading: outer wrapper `sections.append(f"\n## Knowledge Base\n\n{knowledge_context}\n")` (base.py:733) wraps inner `lines = ["## Knowledge Base", ""]` from `_format_knowledge_entries` (retriever.py:58) — heading appears twice when any KnowledgeEntry exists.
7. pipeline_describe_table per table on cache miss: `_fetch_schema_context` (base.py:205) loops `for t in tables: detail = await pipeline_describe_table(...)` (base.py:268-269); reached on every `_build_system_prompt` cache miss (`_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TTL = 60`, base.py:128). Confirmed for the single-tenant branch.

INACCURATE (basis for PARTIAL):
A. The finding states `applies_to_tables` is "'for relevance filtering' per the docstring." No such docstring exists. `models.py:156` help_text is literally "Tables this learning applies to." A repo-wide grep for "relevance" in apps/knowledge returned zero hits. The display-only conclusion is still correct (retriever.py:130-132 only renders it as text), but the quoted docstring is fabricated.
B. The pipeline_describe_table-per-table cost applies ONLY to the single-tenant path. The multi-tenant builder `_fetch_multi_tenant_schema_context` (base.py:306-393) lists views via `workspace_list_tables` and never calls `pipeline_describe_table`. The finding presents this cost as unqualified.
votes.notes: Severity (LATENT / cost-perf) and reachability are correct: the import path is live in the UI and the prompt-injection path runs on every agent turn. Net: the defect is real and reproducible; downgrade to PARTIAL is purely for the fabricated docstring quote (A) and the over-generalized per-table-describe cost claim (B), which a fix author should not take at face value. Worth noting the import view also lacks the READ_WRITE gate that should exist — that authz gap is arguably the more security-relevant sub-claim and is fully confirmed, though the finding files it under cost-perf.

title: TableKnowledge keyed by physical schema-qualified name: refresh orphans annotations, multi-tenant mismatch, stale names injected into prompts
claim: TableKnowledge.table_name stores qualified_name = f'{schema_name}.{table_name}' from the current physical tenant schema. create_refresh_schema mints a new {base}_r{uuid8} name on every refresh and teardown changes the live name, after which dictionary lookups under the new name miss all annotations (silently vanish from UI), while KnowledgeRetriever keeps injecting stale '### old_schema.table' headings into the system prompt (names the SQL validator rejects and that the agent never queries). In multi-tenant workspaces annotations attach to tenants.first()'s physical schema while the agent queries ws_* {prefix}__{table} views — annotation guidance names a schema the agent shouldn't query. Same stored-physical-reference defect class as the known artifacts-broken-by-rename incident; Learning.applies_to_tables/related_tables are bare-string matched, same family.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain from write -> orphan -> prompt injection -> validator, against the actual code.

KEY: How TableKnowledge.table_name is populated (the finding's central premise):
- apps/workspaces/api/views.py:524-527 (TableDetailView.put): `tk, _ = TableKnowledge.objects.get_or_create(workspace=workspace, table_name=qualified_name, ...)`. `qualified_name` is the URL path arg from `path("data-dictionary/tables/<str:qualified_name>/", TableDetailView.as_view())` (apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:18).
- Frontend sends `${schema}.${table}` as that path segment: frontend/src/store/dictionarySlice.ts:218 and :253, where `schema` is the physical schema name from the dictionary response. So table_name is stored as the live physical schema-qualified name. CONFIRMED.
- _get_annotation looks up by the same key: views.py:219 `TableKnowledge.objects.get(workspace=workspace, table_name=table_name)` with table_name=qualified_name (views.py:290, 487). Unique key is (workspace, table_name) (apps/knowledge/models.py:76).

Physical schema name changes on refresh:
- Initial provision is STABLE: `schema_name = self._sanitize_schema_name(tenant.external_id)` => `t_<external_id>`, no uuid (schema_manager.py:66).
- Refresh mints a NEW random name: schema_manager.py:176 `schema_name = f"{self._sanitize_schema_name(tenant.external_id)}_r{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"`. The refresh task marks it ACTIVE (tasks.py:182) then tears down the prior ACTIVE schema (tasks.py:186-195). _resolve_tenant_schema returns the ACTIVE schema (views.py:32-37), so post-refresh the dictionary surfaces the new schema name.
- I grepped every TableKnowledge write site (views.py:219, :524 and the retriever read at retriever.py:70). There is NO code anywhere that updates TableKnowledge.table_name on refresh/rename. So old rows are orphaned: _get_annotation under the new `t_<id>_r<uuid>.table` misses the row stored under `t_<id>.table`. CONFIRMED -> annotations silently vanish from UI.

Stale names injected into the prompt (retriever):
- KnowledgeRetriever._format_table_knowledge dumps every row's table_name verbatim as a heading: retriever.py:77-78 `async for table in tables: lines.append(f"### {table.table_name}")`. No reconciliation against the live schema.
- This is LIVE: build_system_prompt calls it on the agent path — apps/agents/graph/base.py:730-733 `retriever = KnowledgeRetriever(workspace); knowledge_context = await retriever.retrieve()`. So orphaned `### old_schema.table` headings keep getting injected.

Validator rejects those stale schema names:
- mcp_server/services/query.py:31-34 `_build_validator` -> `SQLValidator(schema=ctx.schema_name, allowed_schemas=[], ...)`.
- sql_validator.py:279-288: allowed = {"public", self.schema.lower()} only (allowed_schemas empty). Any other schema -> SQLValidationError error_type="schema_not_allowed". So a query qualified with the stale/old schema is rejected. (Caveat: query.py:47 SET search_path TO ctx.schema_name means bare-name queries work, so the agent normally isn't blocked — but the prompt guidance names a wrong, rejected schema.)

Multi-tenant mismatch:
- workspace.tenant = self.tenants.first() (apps/workspaces/models.py:144-146). DataDictionaryView resolves _resolve_tenant_schema(workspace.tenant) (views.py:245) -> first tenant's physical `t_<id>` schema -> qualified_name annotations key off that.
- But for 2+ tenants the agent routes to the view schema: mcp_server/context.py:104-139 -> QueryContext.schema_name = vs.schema_name (`ws_<hash>`), and validator schema is `ws_<hash>`. The agent queries `{prefix}__{table}` views inside `ws_<hash>` (schema_manager.py:334, 367-374). So the stored annotation `t_<firstTenantId>.table` names BOTH a schema the agent can't access AND a table name (`table`, not `prefix__table`) that doesn't exist in the view schema. CONFIRMED.

Learning.applies_to_tables / related_tables family: retriever.py:130-132 and :95-107 render these bare JSON strings verbatim into the prompt with no lookup. Same stored-bare-name drift family, though "matched" overstates it — they are displayed, not matched against anything.

Reachability: fully wired today. PUT/GET annotation route is live (urls.py:18) and used by the frontend dictionary UI (dictionarySlice.ts). RefreshSchemaView POST is a live user-triggerable route (views.py:325-365, read-write/manage role) that mints the new schema name. The retriever runs on the live agent system-prompt path (base.py:730).
votes.notes:
Verdict CONFIRMED; status correctly LATENT (no harm until a refresh occurs or a multi-tenant workspace exists; single-tenant never-refreshed workspaces have a stable `t_<external_id>` name and are unaffected). Impact correctly correctness.

Two minor precision corrections (do not change severity):
1. "names the SQL validator rejects and that the agent never queries" — accurate for schema-qualified queries, but the executor does `SET search_path TO ctx.schema_name` (query.py:47), so the agent normally issues bare-name SQL and is not hard-blocked at runtime; the concrete harm is (a) annotations vanish from the UI after refresh and (b) the prompt carries misleading/wrong schema+table names (especially multi-tenant, where even the bare table name `table` is wrong vs `prefix__table`). The "validator rejects" point holds only if the agent copies the stale qualified name verbatim.
2. "applies_to_tables/related_tables are bare-string matched" — they are rendered verbatim into the prompt (retriever.py:95-107, 130-132), not matched against any live catalog. The drift concern is valid; "matched" is imprecise.

The "same defect class as artifacts-broken-by-rename" framing is consistent with the codebase's known stored-physical-reference incidents (see context memory on artifacts/rename), and the design here repeats it by keying TableKnowledge on the mutable physical schema-qualified name with no rematerialization hook to re-key on refresh.

title: MCP HTTP server has no caller authentication; teardown_schema/query trust workspace_id blindly; isolation is network topology only
claim: The streamable-HTTP MCP server configures only DNS-rebinding Host checks — no token/secret/principal; auth.py only extracts OAuth tokens. Every tenant-scoped tool (list_tables/describe_table/get_metadata/query/get_lineage/get_schema_status/teardown_schema) resolves context purely from the workspace_id argument with no entitlement check; only run_materialization verifies membership, and even there run_materialization's guard passes for any user when user_id=''. Prod binds 0.0.0.0:8100 on the shared docker network with no proxy, so any co-located process, SSRF, dev port-forward or compromised container can call teardown_schema(confirm=True, workspace_id=<any>) to destroy data or query any schema. cancel_materialization/get_materialization_status accept unscoped LLM-supplied run_ids. Inside chat the args are injected server-side, so exposure depends entirely on deployment network isolation, documented only in a comment.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced every link independently against the live code.

1) No inbound auth on the HTTP MCP server. `mcp = FastMCP("scout")` (server.py:68) is constructed with no `auth`/token-verifier. The only transport hardening is `mcp_server/server.py:909-912`: `TransportSecuritySettings(enable_dns_rebinding_protection=True, allowed_hosts=["127.0.0.1:*","localhost:*","[::1]:*","scout-mcp-web:*"])` — these are Host-header (DNS-rebinding) checks, not authentication. FastMCP is v1.26.0 (uv.lock), which requires explicit auth config; none exists. `mcp_server/auth.py` only has `extract_oauth_tokens()` returning `meta.get("oauth_tokens", {})` — these are *outbound* CommCare/OCS bearer tokens (used in loaders/commcare_base.py, ocs_base.py), not caller authN.

2) Client connects with URL only, no credential: `apps/agents/mcp_client.py:51-53` builds `MultiServerMCPClient({"scout-data": {"transport": "streamable_http", "url": url}})` — no auth header.

3) Tenant-scoped tools trust workspace_id with zero entitlement check. `list_tables/describe_table/get_metadata/query/get_schema_status` all call `_resolve_mcp_context(workspace_id)` → `load_workspace_context` (context.py:83-139), which only maps workspace_id→schema and returns a QueryContext; no user is even passed in. `query` then runs `execute_query(ctx, sql)` (server.py:351). `get_lineage` does `Workspace.objects.aget(id=workspace_id)` (server.py:308) with no membership check.

4) teardown_schema is destructive with no authZ: server.py:801-865 requires only `confirm=True` and a valid workspace_id, then `SchemaManager().ateardown_view_schema` + `ateardown` on every tenant schema. No user/membership gate.

5) Only run_materialization checks membership (server.py:555 `_resolve_workspace_memberships`). Confirmed the guard is bypassed when user_id='': in `_resolve_workspace_memberships` (server.py:509-515) the `qs.filter(user_id=...)` is applied only `if user_id:`, so empty user_id returns ALL workspace memberships → non-empty → passes.

6) cancel_materialization/get_materialization_status take LLM-supplied run_id (server.py:408, 446). run_id is NOT in the injection set (only workspace_id/user_id/thread_id are injected — base.py:504-508), and neither tool scopes run_id to a workspace/user → cross-tenant read (status/pipeline/tenant_id) and cross-tenant sabotage (mark FAILED) by any run_id.

7) Prod network exposure confirmed: config/deploy-mcp.yml binds `--host 0.0.0.0 --port 8100`, `proxy: false`, on `network: scout_shared` with alias `scout-mcp-web`; the "internal only" boundary is documented only in comments (lines 1, 52, and server.py:908). docker-compose.yml mcp-server likewise runs `--host 0.0.0.0 --port 8100` (it uses `expose: 8100`, i.e. docker-network reachable, not host-published).

8) In-chat workspace pivot is blocked, as the finding itself states: base.py:470 does `{**tc["args"], **extra}`, so server-injected workspace_id/user_id/thread_id overwrite any LLM-supplied values. So the primary exposure is direct network reach, not chat — which the finding states accurately.
votes.notes:
Two minor mechanism nuances, neither undermines the finding:

(a) run_materialization is somewhat more protected than the membership-guard bypass implies: it ALSO requires a Thread row matching `id=thread_id, user_id=user_id, workspace_id=workspace_id` (server.py:563-567). With user_id='' that filter won't match a normally-owned thread, so the empty-user-id path can't actually dispatch a materialization against a real thread. The membership guard bypass is real as stated, but run_materialization is not the practically destructive path — teardown_schema (which has NO secondary guard at all) is, and that part is fully correct.

(b) The cancel/get_materialization_status IDOR is reachable in-chat (run_id not server-injected), but practical exploitation needs a valid run UUID, which is hard to guess — so that specific vector is weaker than the unauthenticated-network vector. The dominant, fully-reachable risk is exactly what the finding centers on: any process on the scout_shared docker network can call teardown_schema(confirm=True, workspace_id=<any>) or query(...) with no authentication.

Reachability: live. All eight tools are registered with @mcp.tool() and loaded by the agent (mcp_client.get_mcp_tools); the streamable-http server runs in prod per deploy-mcp.yml. Status DEBT is defensible (deliberate 'internal only, no proxy' architecture with isolation as the single control); LATENT is equally defensible since it's an un-triggered vulnerability gated on network conditions. Impact security is correct.

title: Tenant-membership requirement asymmetric: multi-tenant chat waives the TenantMembership check single-tenant chat enforces
claim: _resolve_workspace_and_membership requires a provider-verified TenantMembership for single-tenant chat but returns success with tm=None for any multi-tenant workspace, requiring only WorkspaceMembership; chat_view skips the per-tenant guard when is_multi_tenant. Member-add only requires sharing >=1 of the workspace's tenants, so a user with TenantMembership for T1 alone gets full SQL read access to T2/T3's data (view-schema queries run as the platform readonly role, not user credentials) in an A+B+C workspace, while a 1-tenant invite does not. The single-tenant TenantMembership check becomes security theater; whether workspace-membership-as-grant or provider-verification is intended is documented nowhere.

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently, entry point to consequence:

1. ASYMMETRIC GUARD — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/chat/helpers.py:106-108: `is_multi_tenant = await workspace.workspace_tenants.acount() > 1; if is_multi_tenant: return workspace, None, True` — for 2+ tenants the function returns success with tm=None after only the WorkspaceMembership lookup (lines 97-102). The single-tenant branch (lines 110-120) requires `TenantMembership.objects.aget(user=user, tenant=tenant)` and returns failure-shaped `(workspace, None, False)` if absent.

2. GUARD SKIPPED — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/chat/views.py:113-114: `if tm is None and not is_multi_tenant: return JsonResponse({"error": "No tenant membership for this workspace"}, status=403)` — the 403 only fires for single-tenant. Route is live: /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/config/urls.py:98 `path("api/chat/", chat_view, name="chat")`.

3. MEMBER-ADD REQUIRES ONLY ONE SHARED TENANT — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/workspaces/api/workspace_views.py:411-414: `shares_tenant = TenantMembership.objects.filter(user=target, tenant_id__in=workspace_tenant_ids).exists()` — `.exists()` over the full tenant list means a user holding TenantMembership for T1 alone passes for an A+B+C workspace. (Contrast: the tenant-ADD endpoint at line 573 does require the requesting manager to hold membership in each tenant added, and workspace creation at lines 172-182 validates all requested tenants — only invited members escape per-tenant verification.)

4. QUERY PATH USES PLATFORM ROLE, NO PER-USER CHECK — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/server.py:71-75 resolves tools purely from workspace_id; /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/context.py:113-139 routes multi-tenant workspaces to the WorkspaceViewSchema using MANAGED_DATABASE_URL platform credentials (no user identity in QueryContext). /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/services/query.py:44 executes `SET ROLE {ctx.readonly_role}` = `ws_<hash>_ro`.

5. ROLE SPANS ALL TENANTS — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:383-405: the view-schema readonly role is granted `GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA {view_schema}` plus `GRANT USAGE` and `GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES` on EVERY constituent tenant schema (loop at 393-405).

Consequence reproduced: a manager of an A+B+C workspace can invite a user who holds TenantMembership only for T1 (step 3 passes); that user then POSTs /api/chat/ (steps 1-2 pass with tm=None) and the agent's MCP tools execute arbitrary read SQL as ws_<hash>_ro across T1/T2/T3 schemas (steps 4-5). The same user invited to a single-tenant T2 workspace would be blocked at member-add AND at chat. Reachable today via live routes; no later guard exists in the MCP path.
votes.notes: One small correction to the claim's framing: "documented nowhere" is slightly overstated — the behavior itself is explicitly stated in the helpers.py docstring (line 93: "multi-tenant workspace; WorkspaceMembership is sufficient"), and views.py:107-108 comments on the mechanism. So the waiver looks deliberate, not accidental. What is genuinely undocumented is the security RATIONALE — why provider-verified tenant membership is required for 1-tenant workspaces but waived for N-tenant ones, which inverts expected strictness (more tenants = more data = weaker check). The "security theater" characterization is fair: the single-tenant check provides no protection a manager can't bypass by adding a second tenant. Note the exploit requires a MANAGE-role insider to perform the invite (workspace_views.py:390), so this is privilege-escalation-via-authorized-invite rather than an unauthenticated hole — consistent with DEBT status rather than BROKEN-NOW, though if cross-tenant isolation is a contractual guarantee, treating it as LATENT would also be defensible. Status/impact as filed (DEBT/security) are reasonable; verdict CONFIRMED with the documentation nuance above.

title: Account merge gate requires a verified EmailAddress the system never produces; local signup bypasses allauth so OAuth-with-same-email can't link
claim: reconcile_existing_user_on_login refuses to auto-merge unless the canonical user has a verified EmailAddress, but ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION='optional' with no production email backend and signup_view creates users via create_user without ever invoking allauth, so no EmailAddress row is created for password accounts and the CommCare/Connect/OCS OAuth flow never creates one either. The verified-email gate is therefore a near-constant False and auto-merge is effectively dead for exactly its target population (password-then-OAuth, or two providers for one human). Duplicate User rows accumulate with TenantMemberships/WorkspaceMemberships split across both rows (two answers to 'what can this user touch'), recoverable only via the operator merge command. The refusal itself is secure-by-design; shipping flows whose accounts can never satisfy the gate is the incoherence. SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION makes provider-asserted emails an auth factor, unverified for the three custom providers.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain in allauth 65.14.0 (.venv) rather than trusting the finding.

GATE (signals.py:104-115): reconcile_existing_user_on_login only runs when user.pk is not None AND not user.email (lines 92-95). On email collision it requires EmailAddress.objects.filter(user=canonical, email__iexact=new_email, verified=True).exists(); else it logs "Refusing auto-merge" and returns. Confirmed wired: apps/users/apps.py:24 pre_social_login.connect(reconcile_existing_user_on_login), and tests/test_social_login_reconciliation.py:210 asserts it.

PASSWORD SIGNUP (auth_views.py:165): user = UserModel.objects.create_user(...) — no allauth call, so no EmailAddress row. models.py:59 confirms email is just a field. So a password-only canonical user fails the verified-EmailAddress gate. CORRECT part of the finding; test_collision_refuses_merge_when_canonical_email_not_verified (reconciliation tests:155) and its docstring confirm this is INTENTIONAL (prevents takeover-via-signup-with-victim-email).

OAUTH FLOW — finding's load-bearing claim is WRONG. config/settings/base.py:225-238 sets VERIFIED_EMAIL: True for commcare, commcare_connect, ocs in SOCIALACCOUNT_PROVIDERS. I traced what that does:
- .venv/.../providers/base/provider.py:133 sociallogin_from_response calls cleanup_email_addresses().
- cleanup_email_addresses (provider.py:196-228) inserts EmailAddress(email=..., verified=False, primary=True) then loops: "if adapter.is_email_verified(self, address.email): address.verified = True" (lines 219-222).
- adapter.is_email_verified (.venv/.../socialaccount/adapter.py:317-341) reads VERIFIED_EMAIL from provider settings; True -> returns True.
- SocialLogin.save() (.venv/.../socialaccount/models.py:320) calls setup_user_email(), which persists the EmailAddress rows via a.save() (.venv/.../account/utils.py:212-214).
So an OAuth login where the provider returns an email DOES create a persisted VERIFIED EmailAddress. The three custom providers (commcare/commcare_connect/ocs/provider.py) don't override extract_email_addresses, so they inherit exactly this path. The finding's assertions "the CommCare/Connect/OCS OAuth flow never creates one either" and "unverified for the three custom providers" are false.

CONSEQUENCE for the finding's headline population (two OAuth providers / OAuth-with-same-email): the canonical created via OAuth HAS a verified EmailAddress, so a second OAuth login with the same email passes the gate and auto-merges. Verified by test_collision_allows_merge_when_canonical_email_verified (reconciliation tests:183) and test_collision_merges_user_and_redirects_session:70. So the gate is NOT "near-constant False" and auto-merge is NOT "dead for exactly its target population."

The genuinely-affected population is the narrow password-signup-then-OAuth case; recovery via the existing operator command (apps/users/management/commands/merge_duplicate_users.py + services/merge.py merge_users). The duplicate-rows-with-split-memberships concern is real for that narrow case (merge.py:_merge_tenant_memberships / _merge_workspace_memberships handle it on operator merge).
votes.notes: PARTIAL, not REFUTED: a real residual gap exists (password-signup-then-OAuth requires operator merge; the verified-email gate can't be satisfied for a pure password account), and the secure-by-design refusal is correctly characterized. But the finding's mechanism is materially wrong: VERIFIED_EMAIL: True (base.py:225-238) + allauth 65.14's is_email_verified/cleanup_email_addresses/setup_user_email chain means OAuth logins that return an email DO create verified EmailAddress rows. That collapses the finding's most-emphasized "two providers for one human / OAuth-with-same-email can't link" claim — that population auto-merges fine. So the "near-constant False" / "dead for exactly its target population" framing overstates scope by roughly half. Severity DEBT (not BROKEN/correctness-emergency) is appropriate; impact is correctness/velocity (manual operator step) for the narrow password-then-OAuth case only. Also note the memory index records this was already triaged and resolved-by-operator-merge with no code change. Caveat: I could not boot Django to runtime-confirm is_email_verified end-to-end (settings not configured in a bare python shell), but the source trace is unambiguous and the codebase's own tests exercise both merge-allowed and merge-refused branches.

title: Hardcoded 'commcare_sync' pipeline fallback in four places returns wrong-provider metadata silently; cascade duplicated 3-4x
claim: Four call sites fall back to registry.get('commcare_sync') when pipeline resolution fails — including for OCS/Connect tenants and multi-tenant view schemas — silently using wrong-provider pipeline metadata instead of surfacing an error. The 'last run's pipeline -> provider pipeline -> commcare_sync' cascade is copy-pasted across views.py (twice), server.py, and a fourth variant in prompt assembly that skips the last-run step, so prompt and tools can name different pipelines for a tenant whose provider mapping changed. apps/workspaces and apps/agents also import mcp_server internals directly, making the 'standalone MCP server' boundary one-directional fiction.

votes.evidence:
Structural claims confirmed; severity/mechanism overstated. The cascade exists in three real config-resolution sites plus one prompt-string variant: mcp_server/server.py:91-101 `_resolve_pipeline_config` (`if last_run: registry.get(last_run.pipeline)` -> `if ts: registry.get_by_provider(tenant.provider)` -> `return registry.get("commcare_sync")`), used by list_tables/describe_table/get_metadata (server.py:159,214,268); apps/workspaces/api/views.py:265-271 (`_get_from_pipeline`) and 445-451 (`_get_pipeline_table`), both ending `if pipeline_config is None: pipeline_config = registry.get("commcare_sync")`; and apps/agents/graph/base.py:240-241 (`_fetch_schema_context`: `if pipeline_config is None: pipeline_config = registry.get("commcare_sync")`) plus the string-default variants at base.py:218 and 740 (`pipeline_name = pipeline_config.name if pipeline_config else "commcare_sync"`).

REFUTED: "wrong-provider metadata, including for OCS/Connect tenants." The final `commcare_sync` step only fires when `get_by_provider(provider)` returns None. PROVIDER_CHOICES in apps/users/models.py:94-98 are exactly commcare/commcare_connect/ocs, and pipelines/ has commcare_sync.yml(provider:commcare), connect_sync.yml(provider:commcare_connect), ocs_sync.yml(provider:ocs) — `get_by_provider` (pipeline_registry.py:102-112 loops configs matching provider) returns a real config for all three. So an OCS tenant resolves ocs_sync, a Connect tenant connect_sync; the commcare_sync fallback is unreachable dead code for any real per-tenant path, not a silent wrong-provider substitution.

PARTIAL on view schemas: only `_resolve_pipeline_config(ts=None)` returns commcare_sync, for `ws_*` view schemas in the MCP describe_table tool (server.py:194 `TenantSchema.objects.filter(schema_name=ctx.schema_name)` is None for a view schema). But metadata.py:212 uses pipeline_config only for `source_descriptions = {s.physical_table_name: s.description ...}`; columns/types come from information_schema (metadata.py:199-207). View physical names won't match commcare_sync source names, so descriptions come back EMPTY, not wrong. The finding's claim that the cascade is "duplicated for multi-tenant view schemas" in PROMPT assembly is false: `_fetch_multi_tenant_schema_context` (base.py:306+) uses `workspace_list_tables(ctx)` (information_schema views) and never references pipeline_config or commcare_sync.

PARTIAL on "prompt and tools can name different pipelines": apps/workspaces/tasks.py:166-172 and 248-275 always select the pipeline via `provider_pipeline_map = {p.provider: p.name ...}; pipeline_name = provider_pipeline_map.get(tenant.provider)`, i.e. identical to get_by_provider. mcp_server/services/materializer.py:188 sets `pipeline=pipeline.name`, so `last_run.pipeline == get_by_provider(provider).name` ALWAYS holds unless the YAML mapping or tenant.provider drifts after a run. The prompt's `run_materialization(pipeline="{pipeline_name}")` instruction (base.py:223) is moot anyway — the run_materialization tool (server.py:521) takes no pipeline argument and always defers to materialize_workspace. Divergence is description-only, never affects which pipeline runs.

CONFIRMED cross-app boundary violation: grep shows apps/agents/graph/base.py:47-49, apps/workspaces/api/views.py:26-27, apps/workspaces/tasks.py:34-36, apps/artifacts/views.py:25-26, apps/transformations/services/executor.py:27 all import mcp_server internals — the "standalone MCP server" boundary is indeed one-directional fiction.

Reachability CONFIRMED: views are live (apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:16,18 -> DataDictionaryView/TableDetailView, both single-tenant via Workspace.tenant=`self.tenants.first()` at models.py:144-146 and `_resolve_tenant_schema`); the three MCP tools are live agent tools; prompt assembly runs every chat turn.
votes.notes: The defect is real as DEBT: a 3-way cascade copy-pasted across server.py/views.py(x2) with a 4th variant in base.py that diverges (skips last-run), plus a confirmed cross-app boundary violation. But the impact is maintainability/velocity, NOT correctness: (1) the final commcare_sync step is unreachable dead code for every real per-tenant path because all three providers have matching pipeline YAMLs; (2) the only reachable commcare_sync fallback (multi-tenant view schema in describe_table) yields empty descriptions, not wrong ones, since columns come from information_schema; (3) last_run.pipeline cannot diverge from get_by_provider under current config because the materialize task selects the pipeline identically; (4) run_materialization ignores any pipeline arg, so prompt/tool "naming different pipelines" has zero functional consequence. Recommend the finding be reframed: extract the cascade into one shared `resolve_pipeline_config(ts, last_run)` helper (already exists as _resolve_pipeline_config in server.py — the views/base.py just don't reuse it), make the commcare_sync fallback either an explicit error or remove it (it masks a genuinely-unconfigured provider, which would be the only correctness trigger), and address the apps->mcp_server import direction as a separate architectural item. Severity downgraded from correctness because the "silent wrong-provider metadata" consequence does not occur under the current provider/pipeline set.
